title: Arbitrary-genus dark soliton gases in the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger hydrodynamics
authors: Marco Bertola, Deng-Shan Wang, Peng Yan, Dinghao Zhu
abstract: The defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger hydrodynamics supports exact dark solitons under finite density boundary conditions. However, the dark soliton gas, an interacting ensemble of dark solitons, has not yet been studied. In this work, we introduce an arbitrary-genus potential of dark soliton gases by considering the limit of the $\mathcal{N}$-dark soliton as $\mathcal{N}\to \infty$. The large-space asymptotics and long-time evolution of this dark soliton gas potential are analytically investigated through Deift-Zhou nonlinear steepest descent approach. The genus-$N$ dark soliton gas potential approaches the genus-$N$ finite-gap solution as $x \to -\infty$ and the background $1$ as $x \to +\infty$. In the long-time evolution, as the self-similar variable $ξ=x/t$ increases, the gas configuration exhibits a cascade of behaviours, passing from unmodulated and modulated genus-$N$ regions and progressively reducing the genus down to the planar region (unmodulated genus-$0$ region). Notably, the evolution of lower-genus soliton gases can be embedded within that of higher-genus gases, exhibiting identical dynamics within specific regimes. This phenomenon is encoded by the underlying spectra. We also include numerical validations, in perfect agreement with the theoretical predictions.
summary: This paper introduces and analyzes the dynamics of dark soliton gases with arbitrary genus in the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger equation. It establishes how these complex soliton ensembles evolve over time, passing through various modulated and unmodulated states. The results are supported by rigorous Riemann-Hilbert analysis and numerical simulations.
main_problem: The paper investigates the construction and asymptotic behavior of arbitrary-genus dark soliton gases within the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger (dNLS) hydrodynamics.
main_result: The authors successfully derive the long-time evolution and large-space asymptotics of higher-genus dark soliton gases, demonstrating a cascade of behaviors through modulated and unmodulated regions.
method: The study employs the Deift-Zhou nonlinear steepest descent approach, Riemann-Hilbert Problem (RHP) framework, Whitham modulation theory, and the g-function approach via a variational formulation.
model_or_system: The system is the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger (dHS) equation, which describes physical contexts such as Bose-Einstein condensates (Gross-Pitaevskii equation) and electromagnetic wave envelopes in defocusing optical media.
key_observables: The amplitude/density of the dark soliton gas potential |q(x, t)|^2 and the phase functions of the soliton gas.
important_parameters: The self-similar variable xi = x/t, the genus N of the soliton gas, the velocity v of solitons, and the spectral parameters (eigenvalues) on the unit circle.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the system is integrable and follows the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger hydrodynamic model with finite density boundary conditions.
figures_summary: Figures include a qualitative map of the x-t evolution plane, numerical simulations of N=1 and N=2 cases, a visualization of the band-gap conversion mechanism, and snapshots of the soliton gas zero level sets.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds from the construction of the arbitrary-genus potential via an N-to-infinity limit, followed by the analysis of large-space asymptotics, the derivation of long-time evolution using RHP and Whitham theory, and finally numerical validation.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for understanding complex, interacting ensembles of solitons in many-body systems like BECs, specifically describing how higher-order structures decay into simpler planar or modulated regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 3: Illustration of the initial potential and time evolution of the genus-1 dark soliton gas in terms of Riemann invariants. The red curve shows the evolution of Re α1 with respect to the self-similar variable ξ according to (1.15). (c) and (d) are two different types of formal degeneration of the genus-1 dark soliton gas.
Figure 4: Band-gap conversion. The red curves are gaps, and the blue curves are bands of the genus-one solution. After the transformation, the roles of the band and the gap are interchanged.
Figure 5: Schematic diagram of solitons concentrated within 2N curve intervals.
